coredata.dc:description: As the world rushes to expedient the growing demands for energy utilization and storage solutions, Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are dominating in almost every sector of the battery systems. Recent research and development in the continuing energy revolution have demonstrated that LIBs are a viable technology for portable gadgets and Electric Vehicles(EV). This is primarily owing to their exceptional performance capabilities and reasonably favorable cell durability. Nevertheless, the optimization of manufacturing electrodes is a crucial factor in guaranteeing the producing top of the line LIBs. The production of LIBs is a complicated system because of the interdependent electrochemical kinetics involved in their chemistry. It has become one of the challenging aspects due to the continuous update of new materials and methodologies. The introduction of digital tools to overcome challenges can optimize these parameters of manufacturing LIBs which are being researched and reaching a phase of implementation in the related industry. Typical state-of-art manufacturing of batteries is a sequence of interdependent steps like slurry preparation, coating and drying, electrode cutting, calendering, stacking, pouch cell formation, electrolyte filling, sealing, and testing, which need precise control. Optimization of the process for each dependent parameter with cautiousness and reorganize them to adopt new innovative battery technologies, which takes a painstaking effort and machine handling to implement. Automation using Machine learning (ML), the Internet of Things (IoT), and Artificial Intelligence (AI) are the ultra-modern approach to the manufacturing process. Digitalization of these techniques provides profitable manufacturing and guides cell prototyping and advanced cell chemistry to the new manufacturing tools in a virtual way. Hence, designing tools and prototyping costs can be reduced. This paper looks at both experimental and computational approaches to make a smooth transition in the process of battery manufacturing.

Loss of consistency within the batch
Might take several hours to mix the slurry.
The power input needed becomes difficult as the mixer size increases.
Greater footprint and additional workers needed to clean the mixers.
Impossible to guarantee, that all components will experience same amount of sheer force to mix
Process time will be faster(can be reduced by4-6 times) compared to batch mixing.
Continuous providing the slurry to coating/drying line.
If a problem arises in the mixing the associated coating and drying lines also need to be stopped.

head.ce:title: A critical review of future aspects of digitalization next generation Li-ion batteries manufacturing process

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electromobility Research Centre, Vrije Universiteit Brussel, Pleinlaan 2, Brussels 1050, Belgium
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Mobility, Logistics and Automotive Technology Research Centre, Vrije Universiteit Brussel, Pleinlaan 2, Brussels 1050, Belgium

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As the world rushes to expedient the growing demands for energy utilization and storage solutions, Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are dominating in almost every sector of the battery systems. Recent research and development in the continuing energy revolution have demonstrated that LIBs are a viable technology for portable gadgets and Electric Vehicles(EV). This is primarily owing to their exceptional performance capabilities and reasonably favorable cell durability. Nevertheless, the optimization of manufacturing electrodes is a crucial factor in guaranteeing the producing top of the line LIBs. The production of LIBs is a complicated system because of the interdependent electrochemical kinetics involved in their chemistry. It has become one of the challenging aspects due to the continuous update of new materials and methodologies. The introduction of digital tools to overcome challenges can optimize these parameters of manufacturing LIBs which are being researched and reaching a phase of implementation in the related industry. Typical state-of-art manufacturing of batteries is a sequence of interdependent steps like slurry preparation, coating and drying, electrode cutting, calendering, stacking, pouch cell formation, electrolyte filling, sealing, and testing, which need precise control. Optimization of the process for each dependent parameter with cautiousness and reorganize them to adopt new innovative battery technologies, which takes a painstaking effort and machine handling to implement. Automation using Machine learning (ML), the Internet of Things (IoT), and Artificial Intelligence (AI) are the ultra-modern approach to the manufacturing process. Digitalization of these techniques provides profitable manufacturing and guides cell prototyping and advanced cell chemistry to the new manufacturing tools in a virtual way. Hence, designing tools and prototyping costs can be reduced. This paper looks at both experimental and computational approaches to make a smooth transition in the process of battery manufacturing.

ce:keyword.ce:text: hierarchical experimental laboratory automation and orchestration

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: With economic development, worldwide energy need have increased fundamentally during the past 50 years [1,2]. It has become pivotal to increase advanced, environmentally friendly power for the productivity of energy [3]. Growing power, consumer electronics, and charged portability fuel interest in versatile power sources, which animate the turn of events and the executives of energy storage devices [4,5]. Since the invention of Li-ion battery technology in 1991, the push for Li-ion batteries has been commodiously increased in different applications because of their advantages like low-discharge conditions, high energy densities, and fast charging, making them an ideal candidate for electrical vehicles (EVs) and in energy storage devices.

The battery electrode manufacturing and assembly processes would have a direct impact on the performance of Li-ion batteries, implying a factor that could directly impede the outcome of improved or upgraded technologies. Battery manufacturing is a major concern for developing constructive energy storage systems, a critical aspect in achieving measurable economics and enhancing the effectiveness of large-scale clean energy applications. To meet these requirements in industrial production, many technologies have surfaced for large-scale and cleaner applications. Standards have begun to change the interaction of conventional manufacturing systems into smart manufacturing plants because of a progression of advancements and advances ready to interface objects, machines, instruments, and workers.

Smart manufacturing [6,7] is becoming a trend in business. Furthermore, in financial development, countless organized machines are being utilized progressively to do battery manufacturing operations. As the LIB manufacturing is interdependent on each process steps and a continuous process, these machines may perform similar or distinct tasks, relying on the output of the previous machines. The correlation between these organised machines can also be effectively engineered to enhance flexibility and customization for certain tasks. Subsequently, the shrewd cooperative energy of organized machines is basic to working on the exhibition of assembling frameworks.

Smart technologies focus on adaptability, quality, effectiveness, and consistency of the creation through the reception of empowering innovations, like the Internet of things (IoT) [8–10], additive manufacturing (AM), Machine Learning (ML) [11,12], Artificial Intelligence (AI) [7,13,14], Big data, and cloud computing. Even though all these are computing concepts, there is a small line of gap between using them in industries that can reduce defects related to the manufacturing process. From an industrial perspective, the significance of the IoT could be acknowledged when the gadgets that are associated with the global machine networks can communicate with one another to provide customer support, intellectual ideas and application systems for businesses, and inventory systems based on the requirements of the perspective venders. They can also perform analytics for businesses. While Big data, Machine Learning (ML) [15,16], and Artificial Intelligence (AI) [14] are authorized to deal with a lot of real-time information created in the brilliant processing plant, thus helping choices in the industrial process. So, industries are now adopting these technologies to meet the demand for innovative solutions and to compete in the market.

In 2009 [17], the concept of IoT to connect the RF identifiers to the internet was originated by Ashton, but in recent years it has been responsible for overseeing system frameworks, representing a relatively oodles of wireless appliances that might interconnect through the internet. IoT devices are mainly classified into four structure blocks [18,19]: sensors/actuators, processors, gateways, and applications. Processor is the brainstorm and operates by processing the data retrieved through sensors. Transportation of the processed data to its appropriate location is being taken care of by the Gateways. Low-power wide-area networks (LPWAN), Bluetooth, and Wi-Fi act as carriers and corresponding standards for the IoT. Sensors acquire data from their surroundings, whereas sensors/actuators provide the processed data. For their surroundings, a suitable application must be used to make use of the acquired data.

These days, due to the technological advancement of ML [20,21] and AI [22,23], the implementation of data-driven strategies has become quite popular in battery management [24]. Lithium-ion battery production and optimization is an extremely complex process, having tightly connected component function affiliations. A viable approach that may evaluate these distinctive factors across the production chain and reap impeccable systemization is thus urgently required. Because battery production involves multiple discrete operational stages, many variables and factors are formed and connected. These process factors will have a significant impact on the quality of manufacturing intermediate products, as a result ultimately affect functioning of the battery. Several trials and errors must now be considered in these intermediary phases to compile and evaluate this data. ML and AI predict the next stages of manufacturing with large amounts of data compiling them.

In this review, we will first assess existing recent advancements in battery manufacturing technologies by building a bridge between the research lab and industrial process in the context of constructing the digitalization of LIBs manufacturing with the present-day technologies like Artificial Intelligence (AI), Machine Learning (ML), and IoT. Then we summarize the process implementation’s possibilities and difficulties in the execution of these new advancements. This paper highlights a brief manufacturing process steps along with simulations for almost all process steps reviewed in the first section, risks and challenges encountered during battery utilization are briefly reviewed in the second section, and digitalization transformation techniques, automation challenges, and future scopes are reviewed in the final section.

The manufacturing of commercial grade Li-ion batteries includes different processing steps. To achieve the high power and energy density of these batteries, designing the electrodes and their processes defines the integrity of the battery. Electrode manufacturing from small scale to industrial scale can be mainly concluded in four steps: slurry mixing, coating, drying, and calendering. These stages impact the electrode homogeneity and robustness of the fabricated cell. The improvement of modern batteries begins with cutting-edge cell technology at the lab scale, where electrodes are designed and tested in half and full cells are being studied through different synthesis routes and slurry preparation methods, whether wet or dry methods, to attain homogeneity of the slurry.

The preliminary step is to explore material properties and compositions, and then they can be transferred from the lab to the production scale. Various parameters impact industrial scale production. These parameters include humidity, processing type, processing time and speed, material addition sequence, viscosity, coating, loading, drying, electrolyte additive, electrolyte filling, porosity, and cutting electrodes. While designing the electrodes, these factors play a vital role. Fig. 1 shows the typical manufacturing process of rechargeable batteries. As the production of LIBs is quite complicated due to many interdependency characteristics, Bayesian Network coupled with Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) might provide a solution to the problem, to root cause analysis network to reduce the failures and process parameters that can be modified according to the process [25,26].
In the following sections, each production step will be reviewed.

While progress in materials and battery design has been ongoing, the principal reason battery research has stayed unaltered throughout the long term is to find the necessary means to make batteries lighter and smaller, and increment in its durability (for example, the lifetime of a battery) and limit their expense while keeping up with safety. Accomplishing these destinations requires an interdisciplinary methodology including material researchers, scientists, physicists, and a developing multitude of applied mathematicians; what's more, modelling researchers. Without a doubt, expanding computational power and improving the algorithms for quantum, atomistic, and continuous models are making a loop between the experimental work and the simulations a reality. Computational simulation approaches have been applied in a range of atomic, molecular, micro, and macro scales. Techniques such as Molecular Dynamics (MD), Monte Carlo (MC), first-principles (FP) calculations, and quantum mechanics (QM) focus on microscale simulations, while the force-field (FF) and phase-field (PF) method simulations focus on mesoscale simulations and finite element (FE) and finite difference (FD) methods [27–33] focus on macroscale simulations. Fig. 2 shows different multiscale methods and the principal applications multiple levels in rechargeable batteries. Automatic material selection needs to be done for the batteries based on battery properties like open circuit voltage, and kinetic barriers. Felix et al. [34] proposed such work on the workflow of Density Functional Theory (DFT) simulations based on Nudged Elastic theory.

The electrode processing of LIBs affects the performance and electrochemical. Mostly, all the processes can be determined as lab scale, pilot scale, and industrial scale processes. The materials should be processed to remove the residual amount of water before going to the production of electrodes. Then all the processes should be done in a dry room that has an ambient temperature of 5-25°C (dew point ((-20) -(-60°C) <100ppm, RH =1%-5%.). The materials can be heated in the vacuum chambers for 10-24 hours at 60-80 °C. The electrode mainly consists of Active Material (AM), Conducting Material (CM), and Binder’s (B). according to recent development popular cathode AMs include Lithium Iron Phosphate (LFP), Nickel manganese Cobalt Oxides(NMC 111, 532, 622, 811) [36–38], and Lithium Manganese Oxide (LMO) while coming to anode either graphite or Lithium Titanium oxide (LTO) and Graphite mixed with of silicon (∼5-10%) to increase energy density. Before the coating on a current collector, the AMs are mixed with CMs and might contain mixed proportions of carbon black, Super-P, KS-6, carbon fibers, CNT, and binders such as polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) mixed with N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) for non-aqueous based slurries and styrene butadiene rubber (SBR) with carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) practiced for aqueous-based slurries. Carbon additives are used to reduce impedance and increase electrical contact, while the binder (typically 1-4wt%) is responsible for improving mechanical properties such as elasticity, adhesion, and stability. The materials’ particle size also has a substantial influence. The average particle size of AM=6-20μm, CM=20-50nm [39–41] for homogeneity of mixing. Depending on the requirement, the battery design and materials used in the company will differ from each other, and the selected materials will be pumped to the mixer through a closed chamber container automatically as shown in Fig. 1. (c)

The next step in the preparation of batteries is slurry mixing and filtration of the prepared slurry, as shown in Fig. 1. (d)& (e). The capability of blending and dispersion of solids in the solvent plays a crucial part in electrode manufacturing: homogeneity of the dispersed slurry, assessed from its rheological studies, mainly depends on the operational conditions, mixing devices, and sequence of addition [42–44]. Recent breakthroughs in the production of electrode slurry processes are classified as wet and dry mixing. The lab scale mixtures (50-100 ml) consist of ball milling, magnetic stirrers, and sonometer; and pilot (1-5 liters) and industrial scale (>10 Liters) planetary mixes, high intensity dry and wet mixers [45–47], universal mixers, and static mixers are used. Various sequences of mixing of the solids and liquids and processing times have been already established depending on the nano and micro-structural properties of the solids. For the Lab scale, mixing time can be ∼280min for wet mixing, pilot scale ∼30-80 min for wet mixing, and ∼20-40min for dry mixing, and for large-scale mixing ∼60min [43,45,48–55] in the wet mixing. The industrial mixers are equipped with a vacuum and maintained at room temperature to maintain the viscosity of the slurry. Slurry mixing methods advantages and disadvantages are shown in table 1 [56–58]. Then the mixture is fed through filters to remove any clumped particles sized more than >40μm [59]. To attain good slurry properties, it should match its rheological properties and the coating parameters.

Multiple computational techniques, ranging from atomic to cell level, were originated to comprehend how the electrode structure and performance in the review [60] were studied. Kinetic Monte Carlo (KMC) [61–63] simulations at the mesoscale level have been used to determine the time sequence and mixing sequence [64]. The below Fig. 3 shows different types of mixing machines’ process steps for slurry mixing.

Electrode coating and drying for solvent evaporation is a crucial step in the next stage of the manufacturing process, as shown in Fig. 1(f), that has a substantial impact on the final microstructure. Various routes are being used to perform the coating. The filtered slurry is fed through pumps to the coating machines on the current collectors. Different industries follow different techniques like roll to roll, slot die, and doctor's blade techniques [43,45,48–53]. Two-sided simultaneous coating machines are available, but most industries do not use them widely due to economic reasons and the structural variations that occur while coating and impact the cell performance. Depending on the requirements, the coating can be done continuously or patterned. Wider coatings are feasible to reduce tension on collectors and rupturing of electrodes while calendering and to achieve higher densities. Coating speed, drying, and calendering conditions also impact the electrochemical performance, life cycle, and high cycling stability. Coating speed also ranges according to the requirements. Lab scale 0.1-1 m min-1, pilot scale 1-10m min-1, and industrial coatings are 20-60m min-1 [45,51,55,66]. Recent technologies are considering higher speeds to reduce energy consumption and increase productivity to reach demand. Considering the volume and mass of the anode and cathode for the capacity matching, the N/P ratio is determined in the range of 1.0-1.2 [67–71]. For balancing of the areal capacities of anode and cathode, the loading of the electrodes may vary according to the application, for graphite electrodes 6-9mg cm-2, NMC-7-18mg cm-2, LMO-12-20mg cm-2 [72–75] is maintained. Recent progress for the thick electrodes has been done by Ritu et al [76] for the aqueous processing for crack free thick cathodes around 5-6mAh cm- 2for industrial application. Many models were put forth to determine the impact of the final electrode micro/mesostructured graphite. LiFePO4, LiCoO2, and LiNixMnyCozO2 have been quantified by 1D, P2D, and 3D-resolved approaches. A 3D-resolved performance model is created to quantify the AM/CB composites of the LIB electrodes multiscale computational methods couple with Coarse Grained Molecular Dynamics (CGMD) [77] model, for categorization reasons in the context of a manufacturing characteristic, its significance, and linkages applied ensemble learnings [78]. To study the highlights of electrode coating parameters and slurry properties a new data-driven approach namely the Gaussian Naive Bayes algorithm [79] has been used to visualize the 2D representation of heterogeneity of coated NMC 811 electrodes. Microstructure parameters like conductivity, specific surface area, tortuosity and diffusivity are significant variables affecting the performance of electrodes. To obtain these parameters between carbon-binder composite and active material-carbon composite spatial locations of the electrodes, a pseudo-2D (P2D), Multiphysics mesh algorithm a 4D-resolved electrochemical model, and CGMD force field (FFs) parameterization models have been developed [80–82]. Liu et al [83]., made a 3D electrochemical model of an NMC-111-based electrode based on the experimental results for electrode formulation and calendering showing a well-connected electronic conductive network formed at carbon and binder domain (CBD) [84] is vital and degree of calendering indicating that increased degree of calendering will decrease interfacial impedance. Xu et al [85]., designed CGMD based AM’s 3D shape particle from the data obtained through X-ray micro-computed tomography to observe the manufacturing parameters which influence the heterogeneity of the electrodes.

The drying conditions also need to be considered along with the coating speed. In the coated electrode which consists of active material, binders, conductive additives, and solvents as a composite, the solvent content must be removed. Factors like sedimentation and flow properties of the slurry, evaporation kinetics of the solvent, solid to liquid ratio, sedimentation and diffusion properties of particles, surface tension of the solvent/slurry and particle interactions [90] determine the drying process. The particulate phases in the slurries will align themselves at the time of drying because of solvent evaporation, which affects the microstructure and performance of the batteries. The solvent used determines the electrode drying process. Also, the type of solvents used in the electrode manufacturing process determines the change of electrode microstructures during the drying process due to the solvent’s parameters like boiling point, viscosity, and evaporation rate [91–93] as they must properly wet the substrates used and crack-free electrodes have to be produced. The first reason is that water contains 72.8mN m-1 of surface tension, which is higher when compared to NMP based, which has 41mN m-1, while aluminum and copper have relatively low surface around 35-40mN m-1 and the second reason is that it might cause excessive particle agglomeration [91]. Homogeneous drying is to be attained for structural integrity and tension of the current collectors. To increase the conductivity of the electrode, the homogeneous distribution of conducting carbon throughout the coated electrode is necessary and a binder to bind the AMs and CCs to the current collectors. To attain homogeneity, dryers are limited to 3 chambers because many variables will come into existence, and the tension of the collectors will also impact. Jaiser et al., [88,94] introduced the concept of 3 stage drying profile based on the impact of adhesion and compact of films on Low and High dry rate’s (LDR and HDR) as shown in Fig. 4 . (c). To attain the quick heating and homogeneous mixing of the particles alongside each other without any accumulation of small particles, high temperature dryers and long-length chambers are not a considerable option. Usually, dry air or nitrogen gas is used for fast drying conditions, and recently UV [54,95–97] or IR rays are used. The dwelling time for each chamber is 1-2 min to attain high throughput of the dryers by varying the 3-stage chamber temperatures as low, high, high [88,98], etc. The dried electrode, which can be collected on the other end, can have a thickness range according to the requirements. On the lab scale, the thickness can be up to ∼110μm, on the pilot scale it varies from 120-160μm, and on the industrial scale it is∼150μm. The general problem encountered during the drying process is binder migration [99]. There is a higher chance of uneven binder distribution happening when the drying process is quicker. For limited drying length and the coatings happening at high speeds, well known drying steps are defined as multistage or two-step drying units. They are used in stages, beginning with low temperatures and progressing to high temperatures. For high coating speeds, two-step or multi-step drying units, with low-temperature rates at the starting point and high at the end of the coating for limited drying length, Fig. 4(b) depicts a schematic representation of solvent evaporation during the drying process, with air fluxes at the top and bottom of the film. As a result of the tension exerted during the drying process, electrode cracking, delamination, and curling may occur [100]. Mehdi et al. implemented a mesoscale simulation approach with LAMMPS [101] as shown in Fig. 4(d). A particle simulator model has been developed to predict particle trajectories for carbon binder mesosphere during drying by varying multiple parameters like slurry, shrinkage ratio during drying, etc., as well as experimental results, to determine mechanical deformations formed due to stress generated during the drying process, which will affect the diffusion and migration parameters. A machine learning-based continuum 1D-modelling approach [102–104] along with experimental details for the drying process has been investigated to predict the drying rates for the coated electrodes, stating that low drying rates are favorable for homogeneous migration while various forces take action like capillary action, diffusion, and convection with different solvents as a predicament, showing water evaporates 4.5 times faster than NMP.

To achieve good adhesion, conductivity, and reduce the porosity of up to 30–40% of the coated electrodes, the electrodes are fed to the calendering machine at a line speed of 3-5m min-1 to 20-30m min-1 [73,74,105,106] to achieve the final thickness of around 90-110μm. The calendering can be either a hot press or a uniaxial hydraulic press in the industry as the results have a narrow optimum on both electrochemical and mechanical characteristics [107]. To prevent the risk of wrinkling of the electrodes, to attain uniform thickness, and other properties and as mentioned earlier, the roller diameters are restricted to 600-1000mm. The maximum line load that can be applied should not reach 1500 Nmm-1 [106]. The electrodes were slit to length and width by mechanical slitting or laser cutting after or before calendering and then transported to the final dryers. Calendering the electrode is important in the manufacturing process as shown in Fig. 1. (h) because it affects the electrode thickness, mass loading per volume, porosity, density, the surface area of the electrode, and cycling performance of the battery [108]. The compactness of the particles and between current collectors and electrode particles increases after compression, resulting in low electron resistance while improving electrolyte phase transportation, electrical and thermal conductivity, and reducing the thickness of the electrode with similar mass loading, increasing the volumetric energy density of the manufactured batteries. Proper compression should be performed during the calendering process to achieve higher conductivity and thermomechanical properties at the same time; otherwise, higher compression can significantly reduce porosity, causing a problem for Li-ion transportation because the active surface area will decrease [109]. Parameters like loading mass, component's physical mean density, and the thickness of a coating are calculated to define the porosity

Defects can also emerge as a result of incorrect calendering and are classified into three types: geometrical, structural, and mechanical defects. When the shape of the electrode changes, geometrical defects arise. Mechanical factors can cause this on both coated and uncoated electrodes and current collectors. Based on the structural properties, structural defects are described further below and classified extensively according to the literature [110,111]. Electrochemical and electrical parameters are greatly influenced by structural defects. For the sake of bridging a connection between experimental and theoretical processes, very few physical models have been constructed with a small number of studied characteristics and compositions to give insights into calendering. A fabric tensor has been applied to discrete elemental models (DEM) by Stershic et al. [112], to analyze the pressurized inter-particle contacts and the particle size distribution (PSD), but it has failed to analyze the increased pressure on the particles. Ngandjong et al. [113] introduced a 3D calendering model by a new DEM method in which they studied both mesoscale and macroscale properties, introducing both carbon-binder domains (CBD) in the active material domain by giving an insight into the link between porosity and calendering pressure curves. Wang et al. [114] introduced a Finite elemental model (FEM) to analyze abrasion and deformation of graphite anode. Gimenez et al. [115] implemented a numerical simulation model using the DEM model (coupled with nanoindentation) to study the single AM particle's mechanical behavior and its interaction with CBDs at the micro-scale level. Laue et al. [116] implemented a pseudo-2-dimensional model (P2D) by improving the classic Doyle-Newman electrode by modelling electrodes at divergent calendering rates and their impact on the cell’s performance. Marc et al. [117] developed a methodology based on experimentally generating in silico electrode mesostructures and ML algorithms for tracking the interdependency of many electrode parameters like particle network, tortuosity, and ionic conductivity of the electrolyte and the surface covered by either AM or CBD on the current collector when calendering is done. These techniques provide a great comprehension of how to ease the manufacturing parameters of the batteries.

Following calendering, the primary step is electrode cutting, as shown in Fig. 1(i), also known as the electrode separation procedure. Depending on the best surface quality of the slit electrodes, performance of the Li-ion batteries varies during the slitting process. Prismatic and cylindrical electrodes are sized by mechanical/automatic rotary knives and die sets, die cutting, rotary knife slitting, or laser cutting (remote or conventional) [118,119]. Both strategies need costly tooling, and over time, the machinery gets wears out, resulting in instability and degradation in addition to inadequate slit quality. This will result in a large amount of heat build-up and an internal short circuit (ISCr) within the cells. Laser cutting, which has been proven as well as widely used in business [120], can overcome the afore mentioned problems. Difficulties in enhancing the surface quality of the silt electrode due to several benefits, such as no tool wear and great energy efficiency. Die finishing [121], punching speed, cutting edge geometry, and thermal and mechanical stress production analysis during cutting are important elements for improving electrode quality. Typically, two types of electrodes cutting have been shown in Fig. 5 . Very less modelling has been done on electrode cutting and its effects [122] until the introduction of laser-based technology as previous technologies are mechanical based, which could result in deformed cutting, edge bending, delamination, and burrs. After the introduction of laser-based cutting-edge technology, many modelling and numerical simulations have been performed from a type of fibre used to using UV laser and IR (fiber/disc) sources in pulsed/cw configurations. Matthias et al. studied different cutting-edge materials related to Li-ion batteries with laser sources which are operated in a pulse and CW modes [123]. A keyhole model has been introduced by R. Fabbro et al., [124] which can study parameters like incident laser intensity, welding speed, or sample material with the help of drilling velocity. Rahul et al. studied the different laser cutting processes for both uncoated and coated electrodes for the extreme slitting speed, slit edge quality, and substantial energy efficiency of the entire process, and based on the process requirements, optimized the slitting speeds ranging few m/min -300 m/min [125]. Lee D. et al [126,127]. studied the interaction model based on 3D- self-consistent laser material.

After electrode cutting, stacking and packing is a crucial step in the production of Li-ion batteries. It’s a critical process step in handling electrodes automatically and assembling them. The current handling process is described as a pick and place operation or cut and place operation with some variable parameters [128]. There are mainly three types of stacking of electrodes, namely, single electrode stacking, winding, and Z-folding. There are advantages and disadvantages to all methods. Assembling a Z-folded composite is presently a well-established method for pouch cell production. To avoid unnecessary sluggish sequential handling tasks, a unique method with a separator has been developed to handle discrete electrodes at continuously feeding speeds and at high-speeds [129], allowing for a considerable reduction in cycle-times and thereby lowering production costs. The manufacturing process is vulnerable to electrode defects because of the lengthy sheets of electrodes. Z-folding techniques fold electrodes into an unending zigzag-shape and give great safety with energy density. However, stacking inaccuracy is a restriction owing to the perfect alignment of electrode-separator assembly. During stacking, various processes and quality parameters must be considered, such as the exact positioning of electrode sheets, proper vacuum generation from vacuum grippers, tape adhesive mechanism, separation of damage-free electrodes, uniform tension and position of the separator, and electrostatic charge generation. Alexander et al., formulated a FEM based 3D model along with the CAD CAM design to validate a high-speed machine for the stacking of the electrodes [130].

The tab welding, pouch-case formation, and sealing steps are the next crucial steps in the production of pouch cells. A pouch case is used to transfer the stacked electrode, which acts as an outer case for the battery. Generally, aluminium laminated film is used. Different sets of dies are used for pouch casing depending on the size and shape of the stack, as shown in Fig 6 (a), below. The tabs are welded to the current collectors using an ultrasonic or laser welding process. Commonly, Al and Ni tabs are used for welding cathode and anode, respectively. The most critical component during welding for providing a long lifetime for pouch cells is sealing tolerance. Proper care should be taken while welding the tab to the stack to reduce the damage and wear and tear of the cell and tab contact point. Generally, a tab consists of PTC material on top of the tab to reduce thermal stress [131] while sealing of the pouch cell. Thermal stress management during contacting, proper pouch stack fitting inside pouch casing (to avoid short-circuiting), sealing temperature, pressure, and time, low contact resistance and mechanical stress during welding, and fatigue strength and tightness at sealing joints are all quality parameters that should be considered during the welding process. All three sides are sealed after proper positioning of the stack in the pouch case, leaving one side for the electrolyte filling. Bayatinejad et al. [132] performed numerical simulations to see the effect of tab geometry and current collector thickness and suggested a new geometry that can decrease the 19.94% heat generation and temperature of the battery, which can increase the lifetime of the cell and prevent thermal runaway of the battery. A one-day vacuum standing step at 80 °C is conducted on the as-prepared pouch cells before the electrolyte filling stage to eliminate moisture from the cell. Liang Xi et al. introduced Finite Element (FE) models to understand the ultrasonic welded electrode’s mechanical performance, which are able to predict energy absorption capability, sheer load, and failure models on the different types of electrodes with unwelded and welded surfaces [133]. A deep learning artificial Neural network (NN) database is introduced to analyse the effects of the ultrasonic spot-welding technique to make a non-destructive technique as a k-fold cross validation by Hwang et al. [134].

During the manufacturing of li-ion batteries, electrolyte filling is the most important and cost-determining aspect. Extreme dry conditions are required to fill the electrolyte and in low-pressure conditions, normally, a 15-20 wt% weak vacuum (<400mbar) is required. Before electrolyte filling, parameters that need to be considered are foaming, metering precision and the percentage of electrolyte evaporation. The vacuum standing method is used to increase the wetting of electrolyte in the electrode by capillary effect at a working pressure of approximately 0.01mbar in 3 different cycling steps. To guarantee homogeneous electrolyte distribution, electrolyte loading can be done in a consistent, continuous or cyclic manner. During electrolyte filling and pre-sealing, quality criteria such as dosing technique, electrolyte delivery system, electrolyte residue, and seal tightness should be examined. Pre-sealing processes are carried out under a vacuum to ensure appropriate sealing and tightness. The geometry of the cells also determines the electrolyte filling, as the diffusion process will take some time to completely wet the cell. Generally, the electrolyte is filled till it reaches half of the designed cell. The filled electrolyte will form a U shape inside the pouch as wetting proceeds and bubbles will be formed. So, the low-pressure sealing will be done to remove the bubbles and acquire fast wettability [137]. Electrolyte wettability is attained in different approaches as it is the main cost-effective bottleneck in the fabrication line of li-ion batteries because it requires several days or weeks at a specific elevated temperature. Hence formation process is the major cost-sensitive step for LIB manufacturing. Cost reduction can be done in two ways (1) lower material cost or (2) more efficient process.

The electrolyte penetration into the electrodes, which have pores and a separator is given by the Lucas-Washburn equation (LWE).

– capillary action’s height at defined time t, λ -pore diameter, σ - surface tension, θ -contact angle, and μ – viscosity of the electrolyte

Numerous studies have been published to reduce the wettability time or effective wettability with less time. Multiple factors are responsible for electrolyte wetting in the literature, such as calendering, temperature, pressure, porosity, and area of the pouch. Diverse means have been reported to investigate electrolyte penetration, and an outline of these techniques is mentioned in the below table 2 .
Pathways of electrolyte infiltration can be understood as follows.
Path1-along edge of electrodes
Path2- along the edge of the separator
Path3-At electrode and separator interface
Path 4 and 5-Infiltration through the electrodes and separator laterally.

The electrolyte wetting process was investigated by Peter et al. [139] using an in-situ chronoamperometry method to reduce the manufacturing costs by optimizing and understanding electrolyte wettability for graphite as an anode. Electrolyte wetting time was calculated for different separators, cell formats [140], and ambient temperature. Furthermore, Habedank et al. [141] studied the electrolyte wetting by in-situ neutron radiography for a different set of electrodes, i.e., conventional electrolyte with 30% porosity, conventional electrodes with 40% porosity, and laser structured electrode with porosity of 30%. Wetting time was reduced by one order of magnitude by using laser structured electrodes as compared to conventional electrodes, and also indicated that large area pouch cells will take more time to achieve the full wettability of the cells. A 3D lattice Boltzmann model was made to investigate the effect of electrolyte filling at the pore scale to understand the filling parameters, physicochemical, and structural properties and their results were used for optimization of process and electrode design [142]. A. Shodiev et al [143]., studied the infiltration of electrolyte in the electrode architecture using the Lattice Boltzmann Method (LBD). This is used to analyse the saturation of electrolyte for different porous architectures of electrodes as a function of time. C. Jin et al [144–146] developed a 2D imaging techniques to determine the wetting process of calendared and uncalendared electrodes and the separators backing through both experimental and theoretically. First, they proposed the change of electrolyte system from EC-DEC to EC-EMC system, then increase in the salt concentration and then for separators the system of inplane and through plane wetting process. The wetting rate is heavily influenced by the separators' pore size, porosity, and microstructure. Electrolyte in-plane wetting has only been observed in wet-process separators. Increasing separator porosity and pore size increases through-plane wetting rate significantly. Furthermore, ceramic coating has been demonstrated to promote in-plane wetting while decreasing through-plane wetting.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Challenges and risks for the manufacturing of batteries
ce:section.ce:para: Contrasted and past battery-powered batteries, lead–acid batteries, for example, rechargeable li-ion batteries have high energy and power densities, specific capacities. These benefits enable Li-ion batteries to have high-current usage and long-haul activity, in needed by numerous individuals of the present versatile electronic gadgets, cells, PCs, and electric vehicles (EVs). Even though having numerous advantages, some security concerns regarding Li-ion batteries usage. Assuming the rechargeable battery is short-circuited or at elevated temperatures, there is a high probability of occurring exothermic reactions, bringing about a self-driven temperature increase termed "thermal runaway," resulting in the catching flames and blasts of the battery. Numerous reported incidents are also there. Li-ion battery-placed gadgets have likewise been referenced in fire-related occurrences, for example, notepad PCs, hoverboards, and electronic cigarettes. The related foundations for the Li-ion battery’s occurrences change. Overcharging, mechanical maltreatment, internal short circuits (ISCs), and assembling defects can all bring about a battery fire/explosion [147]. These problems will be classified in this section.

Saxena et al. [148] explored e-cigar occurrences and discovered that the related market isn't managed properly. Ad-hoc or potentially faulty batteries are plunging into the market, which expands the danger of Li-ion battery blasts. As battery manufacturing consists of different complex parts combined, five important components are at fault if treated or handled improperly. They are the current collectors, the separator, the anode, the cathode, and the electrolytes. Of these components, the electrolyte and the separator are very prone to temperature compared to current collectors and cathodes. Carbonate-based electrolytes at higher voltages or high operating temperatures or during short-circuit (dendrite formation) can cause a fire.

Under operational conditions, very few batteries discharge because of some complex compound reactions, and an exothermic reaction occurs. The generated heat during these reactions tries to dissipate into its nearby surroundings. The internal temperature of a battery rises when the temperature isn't adequately scattered or an external temperature is applied, hence, speeding up the exothermic response. 20–40°C is the ideal working temperature for Li-ion batteries. At temperatures, when lithium gets deposited, beneath 0°C it may turn out to be irreversibly harmed in the charged state. In the most pessimistic scenario, an ISCr might happen. At elevated temperatures beyond 60°C most li-ion batteries are inactive also the pressure escalates inside the battery, combustible gases are delivered, and cells might explode. The low-boiling point of the electrolyte starts to increase and lead to degradation, and the strain escalates to levels at which the cell bursts. Near 130°C, the separator pores are made of polypropylene (PP), polyethylene (PE), or PE/PP close. The separator dissolves and empowers direct connection between cathode and anode, prompting additional heating because of the short out. Therefore, the self-impetus temperature elevates. At 150–250°C, a small amount of anode materials starts to break down, which may increase heat and O2 in an exothermic response and start thermal runaway. Therefore, the anode starts decomposing and mixes with electrolytes, which also leads to pressure, gas, and temperature rise. Thus, the battery will expand and take the initiation to explode.

Electrode manufacturing defects may cause the explosion of the batteries. While fabrication, it is impossible to avoid some defects, they arise due to the following: non-uniform coating, agglomeration, pinholes, cracks, contamination of particles, bending of electrodes, [149] etc. Agglomerates at the coating side can be given rise if the proper slurry preparation has not been done, either by not giving the adequate required time for homogeneous mixture or not using the appropriate slurry feeding rate. As pinholes may arise on the coated electrodes if there are bubbles in the slurry, as they are very small, we cannot see through naked eyes generally. When the coated electrodes are being dried at different heat rates at elevated temperatures, their bubbles will pop up, leaving a micro uncoated space on the electrodes, which can come in direct contact with electrolytes and thus cause damage to the cells and may cause a decrease in capacity. Metal particle contamination is sometimes unavoidable because of the instruments used multiple times for production because continuous usage of these instruments sometimes causes corrosion of their body parts, so it might occur. The most important defect we need to consider is the non-uniform coating, which leads to damage to the whole process and may cause heavy costs, time, and energy. This may arise due to slurry feeding errors, serious malfunctioning of the machine, or tension of the foil while coating. Cracks are also an extended part of the non-uniform coating that will dissolve in the electrolyte and when charged or discharged, these may cause an internal short circuit (ISCr) of the whole cell. Many manufacturing components need to be considered during electrode manufacturing to get rid of these defects.

In September 2016, there was a massive recall of all the Samsung Galaxy Note7 mobile phones as there were many cases of battery explosions and overheating. When the analysis has been done on the root cause of such mishaps, interestingly many manufacturing defects have come into the limelight. Also, the iPhone 8 at a similar time showed battery swelling. Melanie et al. [150,151] looked deeper into note 7 and found out that mainly four defects during manufacturing caused the incident. The defects are listed as follows; a) Near the anode tab, there is an unfolded anode coating; b) missing insulating tape; c) no proper welding on the cathode tab; and d) a thinner separator is used, and the ceramic coating has delaminated from the separator. Another such incident is the Fitbit Flex 2 fitness tracker, where the delamination of the anode coating of the jelly roll sticks to the separator. It happened due to the applied elevated bending stress on components up to 3mm diameter, when the cell is jelly rolled.

An electrolyte is a combination of different organic solvents and Li salts. The electrolyte of a Li-ion battery is combustible and largely composed of LiPF6, LiClO4 or other Li salts. The electrolyte vanishes and releases from the battery cell, when a high temperature is attained which might touch off right away. The gases formed are very toxic and when the blast occurs, the blast transmits an assortment of poisonous substances, including CO (flue gas) [152], phosphoryl fluoride (POF3), hydrofluoric acid (HF), phosphorus pentafluoride (PF5), alongside heat and fire. Lithium likewise responds to the general mugginess of the air due to its great hydrophilicity.

Another reason for the damage/fire catching is the negative/positive ratio unbalance of the materials used. Li-ion batteries are made of stacked multi-layers of porous electrodes [153]. In multi-layer pouch cells, a perfect balance of the capacities between each pair of electrodes is the main point that cannot be overlooked because it ensures the safety of the battery and avoids overcharging of the anode. In case left unnoticed, there are possibilities of forming lithium plating and ISCr or another possibility is cathode over-discharging. This leads to total crumpling of the crystal structure and less possibility of Li-ion intercalation due to vacancy loss. This leads to a drastic reduction in the capacity of the designed cell.

For example, to match the anode-specific capacity of the general graphite electrode, which has a 372Ah/kg theoretical capacity, which is double the amount available in cathode NMC electrodes, cathode electrodes need to be loaded at double thickness to match the areal capacity, otherwise there arises a problem of kinetics and mass transport properties. As a result, searching and new design chemistry for high-capacity cathodes began. Also, minimization of inactive materials in the battery is being considered to enhance cell energy density. One possible way to reduce the inactive materials is to reduce the current collector thickness and increase coating thickness, but this further raises the concern for mass transport, and the designed electrode should have high engineered porosity.

Also, Lithium-ion cells rely heavily on the precision with which the positive and negative electrodes are aligned during assembly. Negative electrodes with a larger surface area/ oversized electrodes have a higher tolerance for misalignment. The effect of an oversized negative electrode on the efficiency of charge and discharge cycling was studied by T. Dagger et al. [154] This type of cell design is preferable since it guarantees full electrode overlap. However, the charge/discharge cycle performance was drastically impacted by the apparently inactive (but in actuality active) overhang of the negative electrode. Specific capacity losses, in particular those occurring over a longer period when the battery is in a charged condition, rose as the overhang area was systematically raised.

Many studies have been conducted in the literature on various cell parameters, fabrication processes, and cell designs during internal short circuits (ISCr), which affect thermal performance. This implies that when a non-coating area of the cathode comes in contact with the anode surface, there is a chance of thermal runaway, and the batteries are extremely vulnerable during that time. The ISCr events can be controlled by controlling the SEI layer stability and heat dissipation of the anode. The ISCr heat energy is solely enough to raise the localized temperature to around 200°C. ISCr could account for a significant portion of the Li-ion battery failure mechanisms in portable gadgets, despite the exceedingly unusual incidence of thermal runaway during ISCr. Potential techniques for protecting Li-ion cells in the event of ISCr thermal runaway include flame retardant [155] electrolytes, high thermal materials, or thermally stable active materials. Uneven electrode coatings might be responsible for the soft shorts. Particularly for large format Li-ion cells which are being utilized in EHVs and EVs, a ceramic coating on the separator with a thickness of around micro layers might lower the possibility of a thermal runaway at times of ISCr. Many suggestions and experiments are still ongoing and under consideration for the quality control of the batteries.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Digitalization of the manufacturing of batteries
ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, electric vehicles have been dominating the automotive industry. It is estimated by the Bloomberg NEF, the future will hold the most vehicles that run on Li-ion batteries within 20 years [156]. Despite the great improvement in LIB technology and research, there are still some limitations that cannot be exceeded. Excessive research on the anode and cathode materials and cell design to overcome these limitations is being done, and some alternative ways are also being established by industry. All this battery research data and the steps are taken through a trial-and-error approach, the economics of battery manufacturing costs, cell operating conditions, materials data, and analysis data have so much data which can be used in the battery industry or manufacturing process to save costs in the production of a battery manufacturing facility. Battery research and R&D greatly benefit from faster and controlled handling of the generated data. Thus, digitalization techniques to consolidate the data and drive us through an intelligent way to the manufacturing process and new research within the scope of Industry 4.0 are being presented.

Transforming theory and computational approaches to guide the experiments as well as manufacturing through a systematic and careful design approach can be established. Recently in-silico tools have been developed to automatically guide and analyze the thousands of materials data and accelerate them if necessary. Due to excessive research on batteries, numerous papers are published yearly, and there is a vast amount of data provided both experimental and simulations. Europe’s Battery 2030+ [157] has created a roadmap to Implicate all this data and make a useful tool assisted with AI and Machine learning based technology for the industrial approach with projects like BIG-MAP [158], Digital TWIN [159], Self-healing materials [160] (BAT4EVER [161]), DEFACTO [162], ARTISTIC [81,85,163,164] and Sensors for large scale battery production. All these projects are based on sophisticated and high-throughput material design for giga-scale battery production. The digitalization of these plays an important role shortly. Mainly the simulations these days is to compliment the experimental results and drive them in the right way because all the experimental approaches are going through a trial and error approach, which creates so much data if transferrable. To reduce these steps, AI/Machine learning tool assisted or IoT based approaches are being established for these multiscale simulations. In Li-ion battery manufacturing Automation/Industry 4.0 is the integration of these advanced computational tools and electrochemical experimental techniques to guide us from basic material discovery to process optimization and high reproducibility [165–167]. To get academia and industry into the same domain, a public data repository or datasheets are needed to identify the process parameters and ensure proper manufacturing [168]. A well-automated laboratory or high-tech laboratory in academia is still like a needle in a haystack. Recent information regarding self-monitored robotics integrated with AI to research, design, conduct experiments, and collect data to revitalize the experiments is being conducted in both industry and academia for different backgrounds. For the autonomous discovery of thin film materials, a self-driving laboratory [169] and to discover different materials a mobile robotic chemist [170] has been established.

A multiscale tool is essential to integrate both experimental and simulation data for manufacturing the battery, which can have benefits like automation, complexity reduction, reusability, scalability, data management, data reusable, and manufacturing. The battery's main components are the anode, cathode, and electrolyte, but the manufacturing or prototyping of the battery includes six main steps consisting of material design, electrolyte, interface, cell balancing, and lifetime as shown in Fig. 8. Each one contains enormous research and data. The digitalization process, which has been successfully implemented in several sectors, is defined as the process of conversion of analog characteristics into digital values, allowing for electronic and informational data transmission, storage, and processing. Although a few of the battery production process’s digitalization ambitions are aspiring, there is a desire for it to advance into nearly flawless automation, harmonious human integration, and automated decisiveness, which would result in “smart” battery production factories and entirely integrated starting with raw materials design to completed manufacturing.

Using AI, ML, and Multiphysics modelling techniques [171,172] in material synthesis, manufacturing, and cell configuration might be of great significance in accelerating novel designs concerning needed objectives (e.g., lifespan, stability, energy density) and efficiency (e.g., by making sure that self-healing capabilities of the materials and the sensing technologies are amalgamated in advanced battery technology). The development of innovative manufacturing processes may be aided by hastening the development of current and future manufacturing processes concerning the cost of manufacturing, feasibility, cell chemistry, and environmental effects (e.g., degrading energy consumption).

For a long time, Li-ion batteries have been the focus of study for many years to increase not only cycling stability as well as energy density. At the moment, standard cell design relies on the trial-and-error method to correlate design parameters and production specifications depending on the performance of the cell, necessitating long-drawn-out experiments. For this reason, computational simulations and characterization are necessary for researchers to have a superior grasp of the processes at play in physical domains and at various dimensions of the cell. High computing expenses for the most precise computational models and lack of knowledge about how events at physical domains and multi-dimensions interact, and eventually affect the performance of battery are the key obstacles to their practical implementation as a working foundation in the industry. To deliver ultraprecise findings at reasonable processing expenses and enable widespread usage, computational tools with highly sophisticated numerical techniques are required. Therefore, linking conventional physics-base models to sophisticated optimization techniques in AI frameworks is critical for optimizing cell design in a way that is both efficient and sensible way. However, to determine the best cell designs, the design process must encompass all essential designing factors as well as the effects on the performance of cells determined by various parameters as shown in Fig. 7 . Dealing with such a large range of factors experimentally may be a "nightmare," and simulation-based techniques are very important since they enable comprehension of the entire process in multiple dimensions and domains. So, a database for the batteries is needed to compile all the data and form standard parameters [173].

If we have a clear insight into experimental and theoretical knowledge of structural- property correlations and electrochemical-related mechanisms, we will be able to develop invulnerable batteries faster with high energy densities and extended lifetime [174]. As shown in Fig. 7, each process step of the battery consists of some dependent parameters. At the initial step of making a slurry, finding the suitable materials chemistry which can be safer and has high capabilities is the crucial step for the manufacturing, then mixing time and sequence, and viscosity will only generate a large set of data points to combine and compile the proper set of experiments. Formulation and optimizing these parameters require machine learning or AI guidance which has been already being already claimed by an industrial expert in the revolution of Industry 4.0 [175]. In a similar manner coating, drying, and calendering processes have been discussed in the previous sections. The design of electrolytes and the study of an electrode-electrolyte interface is less explored in the computational area. Recently these areas are also being exploited more for a better understanding of the batteries [176–179]. Adarsh et al [180]., explored autonomous robotic coupled experiments for an effective automated system “Clio” for non-aqueous electrolyte optimization experiments for electrolyte conductivity. An automated electrolyte dispenser named Sidekick [181] is a piece of desktop equipment that can be used in academia. The solid electrolyte interface (SEI) is also an important parameter for the safe operation of the battery. A. Bhowmik et al [182]., proposed an inverse design of battery interphases through experimental and multi-scale modelling to better understand these interfaces at both anode and cathode sides.

Almost all physics-based models implemented to optimize battery cells are based on Doyle's pseudo 2D (p2D) method, which combines Newman et al. [183] porous electrode theory and the concentrated solution theory which has become ground modelling structures in the simulation for the Li-ion batteries. Anode and cathode thickness, particle size, porosity, and numerous other significant electrode parameters have all been optimized using the P2D model. Mehdi et al. [184] designed a user-friendly tool to work on battery design and can compare it with the experimental results. DEM models [185] have been established to study the microstructural properties when calendering has been done to know electrical and ionic conductivity parameters.

At large-scale (Industrial)production the modelling parameters will change, so an ML modelling [186] has been established recently to adapt to these new changes including process parameters and performance and has been reported. The introduction of ML simulations became a huge benefit to the manufacturing industries as it takes very less time maybe within a few seconds than the traditional computational methods take a huge time from hours to days which also reduces computational costs. Online monitoring tools and virtual visualization of the battery manufacturing process can assist to increase in profits and increment in production for cell prototyping and optimization. Furthermore, these developed tools will help in the redesign and help reduce the cost of redesign tools, and suggest the tools for improvement physically. Thus, the digitalization techniques can help Gigafactory productions through real-time monitoring, product dependability, and quality.

Due to the industrial revolution from time to time, and the manufacturing process defined steps the communication with the machine and user interphase has also taken a tool in the increment o the manufacturing processes. Sensing the variables, when smart sensors and their networking technologies are used in the production environment for control, monitoring, and diagnostics as well as data/information collecting has the potential to dwarf all of the gains manufactured by the industry using conventional sensors. With its high sensitivity, capacity to self-calibrate and compensate for nonlinearity, diagnostic modes, digital preprocessed output, self-checking, low power consumption, and interoperability with computers and other subsystem components, smart sensors may considerably improve automation and dependability. There is a significant gap between conventional production system models and the computational models necessary to appropriately define manufacturing systems integrated with smart sensor networks. Generally, sensors used in manufacturing technologies are for monitoring and diagnostics, where monitoring is defined with certain steps and let the machine work on those predefined activities, and diagnostics take place in the event of mishandling happened. Intelligent manufacturing's progress has a substantial and long-lasting impact on futuristic global production. The performance, transparency, management, controllability and quality of manufacturing processes in the IoT era are improved through smart factories grounded on Industry 4.0, which combines physical and cyber technology (IoT). Advanced low-cost sensor technologies are critical for industrial organizations and supply chains to collect data and use it to improve performance. Divergent sensors which work as low-power or low-cost sensors provide considerably extended data gathering on various devices throughout the manufacturing process [187–189]. The majority of firms still lack an in-depth understanding of the topic, despite the fact that much study has been done with an emphasis on evaluating the effectiveness, practices, and use of smart factories.

Expanding the battery capacity quality, reliability, and life call necessitate precise multisensory monitoring of the battery's operational state at the interface, cell, and system levels [190]. Battery manufacturing consists of numerous dependent variables parameters: physical, chemical, interfaces, electronica, and ionic transport properties, etc., as shown in Fig. 8 . In working conditions to meet the demands of the end user all these parameters are further affected and are brought to their limits. As a result, the lack of real-time monitoring data to explain the intricacy has met a stumbling block in the creation of an effective Battery Management System (BMS). Now a days, a BMS generally monitors system-level metrics such as temperature, current and voltage to predict battery quality reliability, life, thermal runaway, and safety. Although temperature monitoring is critical for improving cycle life and battery lifetime, it is not yet detected directly at the cell level for EV applications. Furthermore, the temperature within the cell and its huge gradient across terminals are neglected under certain operating circumstances, often leaving battery packs over-engineered to avoid the danger of early failure, at the expense of more than 20% of the genuine cell capacity. The sensors used to monitor the battery are all attached to the cell’s outer surface rather than the cell itself, along with other electrochemical devices. To reduce these drawbacks, for the past 5 years research is going on the concept of Industry 4.0 or Internet of Things (IoT) as short-distance monitoring through Wi-Fi or optical sensing through fibre optics. Also in situ analysis using X-rays, IR, SEM, NMR [191–194], etc., are being established. All these techniques convert the signals either from physical, chemical, pressure, temperature electrochemical, or mechanical to digital data, and optics are used for the visual inspection by the multisensory systems. Different optical fibres like Fibre Braggs Grating (FBGs) [195], titled-fibre Bragg gratings sensors (TFBGs) [196] or long-period fibre gratings sensors (LPFGs) [197], unsheathed optical fibres [198] are being used for the sensory applications. In short, optical fibre technology integrated with AI programming is playing a crucial part in the online monitoring and manufacturing of batteries.

Big data, cloud computing, AI, and IoT are some examples of disruptive technologies that are emerging in the realm of information and communication technology (ICT). The fourth stage of industrial production (i.e., Industry 4.0). are paving the way by these technologies, transforming the manufacturing and enabling the merging of the real and digital worlds via cyber-physical systems (CPS). The extensive use of CPS in production contexts makes manufacturing systems smarter. These technologies are penetrating the manufacturing business, making it smarter and better equipped to solve contemporary difficulties such as increasingly tailored requirements, greater quality, and rapid market entry [13,199]. One of the primary objectives for achieving the goal of Industry 4.0 is to enable interconnectivity among a broad range of devices [200]. This requires a big volume of data that may be utilized to guide manufacturing choices [201]. However, ensuring data interoperability across devices is one of the most difficult obstacles in adopting this system. Acceptance of applicable standards for the sharing of models and cyber-physical assets [202], as well as the creation of specific data tools for the battery sector, can help to overcome this obstacle.

Automatization of the battery process can be achieved through the integration of these technologies and forming a database, by combining Multiscale computations and AI models of electrochemical performance through data mining or text mining [203] to form a cloud storage database using Chemdata Extractor [204], and enabling the interconnection between the multiple machines. A schematic overview of the protocol of the process can be depicted in Fig. 8.

The idea of automatic battery assembly technology in the laboratory will create a baseline for the pilot lines. Fuzhan et al [205]., proposed the same idea of automation of the laboratory by integrating synthesis, characterization, etc. within the Hierarchical experimental laboratory automation and orchestration (HELAO) framework. AutoBASS [206] is an autonomous battery assembly robotics that can be used for coin cell assembly. Introduction of Battery Passport [207] cloud-based storage provides problems affecting the performance and life of batteries, by monitoring real-time electric vehicle batteries. Online platforms like ARTISTIC [208] and BattMo [209] are coming into existence for a better understanding of battery manufacturing which is run through ML and AI-based technology.

The idea of digitalized battery production eventually necessitates some standard machine-oriented languages for representing battery data, based on commonly shared concepts. Ontologies are an attractive tool for meeting this demand, and efforts are underway in the battery research and development community to build completely open battery cells and manufacturing ontologies.

The present state-of-art Li-ion battery manufacturing process faces a multitude of problems to produce the best quality batteries. Another parameter to take into consideration while manufacturing is debris as Giga factories want to have less time consuming and wastage and in a profitable economic way. The current battery manufacturing process has always followed trial and error experimentation. Currently, established methods are well defined and is hard to adapt to new technologies. Recent progress in new battery technologies like Li/Na-S, Na-ion, Metal batteries air batteries, and Solid-state batteries must accommodate the old technologies, which needed a complete change of the system. As explained in previous sections, coating and drying are the most cost-consuming processes while stacking and electrolyte filling are time-consuming. For the complete autonomous process, the introduction of modelling and AI-assisted technologies can help in creating complete data-driven and physics-based robust tools or models to assist them in the design, development, and validation of the new battery technologies into the market.

All in all, to overcome the manufacturing difficulties, the different domain experts from academia and industry should consider the following possibilities to make a user-friendly tool to completely adapt to the new revolutionized manufacturing process:
a)
Development of a digitalization tool comprising AI algorithms for validating the physics-based and multiscale models for the long-term plan to integrate different next-generation manufacturing technologies [210] not only related to batteries in conjunction with multiple fields.
b)
Assisting in the second life of the batteries, recycling procedures, battery swapping, fast charging points [211], life cycle assessments (LCA), and battery thermal management systems.
c)
Application-oriented manufacturing process through database management through data visualization and virtual manufacturing.
d)
Development of tools for the processing of waste management and recycling methods [212,213] for the spent Li-ion batteries

The inevitable future of battery manufacturing lies behind the digitalization of the process steps via so-called Digital Twins as digitalization of the battery manufacturing processes will have a considerable benefit on product quality, efficient use of resources, thus production time and cost. Digital Twins require extensive data usage and handling from the very beginning. These data need to be a true representative of the physical processes and valid within the concerned timeframe. Nowadays, data security & protection is usually favored as a result of the wide exposure of data through different channels such as the internet and social media. Besides, with the rapidly changing technologies, data validity period is getting shorter. In this regard, there are several aspects to put more emphasis on to meet the expectations and ambitious goals in battery manufacturing digitalization.

First of all, there is a need for a comprehensive approach during data generation. All of the data discussed in different sections of this article - from submicron level towards macro level - should be properly generated and stored. Data must be FAIR, which is Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable. FAIR data is the key tool for the successful implementation of digitalization methods and to ensure interoperable data with respect to data standards into battery manufacturing processes improving the fidelity level.

Batteries are a hot topic nowadays and a considerable amount of research is being performed in every aspect of battery manufacturing such as material selection, electrode preparation, assembly processes and post-mortem. Accordingly, shelf-life (the time interval that the process and/or materials are valid for large-scale production) of the existing processes are rather short compared to the already established processes. This short shelf-life puts a stress on data validation since model development, simulation and validation also requires time and poses a threat that validated models have very short use period to support the physical production.

Considering all, digitalization of battery manufacturing requires a comprehensive, multi-disciplinary look from the data generated to the digital twins. Every step should be inter-connected, feeding each other to accelerated progress and preferably with automated data generation. Digital twins should be flexible and capable of successful implementation of new battery materials, battery chemistries. Improved transferability of digital twins from small-scale production to large-scale production is required to support prolonging the shelf-life of the models.

In this review, we aim to identify numerous challenges and approaches that are interesting to investigate concerning the existing manufacturing process of the Li-ion batteries along with multiscale modelling methods for each process step for Industry 4.0. We identify eight processing steps using slurry, coating, drying, calendering, electrode cutting, tab welding, stacking, electrolyte filling, and pouch forming for the adoption of autonomous ML techniques to apply to LIBs manufacturing. Because of the development of new technologies and the current trend of digitalization, the battery industry is quenching for high-quality battery production with minimal delivery times. It has made the industries adapt to the industry 4.0 approach. Digitalization helps in reducing manufacturing costs, and human error and increases the production speed if adapted.

All authors contributed to researching data, discussing content, and writing and reviewing/editing the manuscript. All data used within this research can be found within the cited works, and included for full transparency of the work.
